content: The single most persistent and dangerous misconception beginners face when working with macros across crate boundaries is treating #[macro_export] as if it were a standard library item like an enum or function that can be imported via the use statement, leading to confusion when they see errors about missing tokens or invalid syntax. This happens because the documentation often presents #[macro_export] and simple macro usage side-by-side without emphasizing that these are fundamentally different mechanisms rooted in how the compiler parses code before it becomes a source file for the user. The core truth is that #[macro_export] does not export a token you can import; instead, it attaches the macro definition directly to the crate root itself, making the macro available implicitly to all functions within that same crate during compilation, but completely invisible and unusable in any external crate unless you explicitly re-export its tokens. The analogy of building a house works well here: if you write the blueprints for a window at your home's foundation using #[macro_export], those blueprints are locked inside your walls; neighbors cannot walk through to look at them or use them without special permissions, whereas placing a window in your neighbor's yard would require you to physically build it there. In Rust terms, this means when you define fn my_macro() { ... } with #[macro_export] in the std library crate, that definition becomes part of the crate's internal logic and cannot be accessed by another crate like serde_derive unless you manually replicate its tokens or use a mechanism like proc-macro crates. The pre-2018 era introduced #[macro_use], which acted as a global import directive for all macros in an extern crate, but this was never designed to handle the specific case of exporting a macro from one crate to another while maintaining proper scoping rules; it simply listed names to be imported into the current scope without validating whether those definitions were actually exported with #[macro_export]. The 2018 edition changed everything by introducing the use statement for macros, but this only applies if you have manually written out the macro tokens in your source file or are using a proc-macro crate that generates them; you cannot simply do use std::my_macro to bring in a macro defined elsewhere because #[macro_export] binds it exclusively to the defining crate's namespace. When re-exporting macros across crates, the correct approach is to explicitly write out the macro tokens in your public API module or create a proc-macro crate that generates those tokens on demand, ensuring the receiving crate can actually parse and expand them without encountering tokenization errors. Beginners often try to bypass this by assuming #[macro_export] magically exports the macro globally like std::println, but Rust's design intentionally keeps macros local to their defining crate unless you take active steps to expose them, preventing accidental pollution of other crates' namespaces while allowing precise control over what gets published as part of your public API. The key takeaway is understanding that #[macro_export] creates a private binding within the containing crate, so any attempt to import it externally without re-exporting the actual tokens will fail at compile time with confusing error messages about unknown identifiers or invalid syntax, which stems from the compiler never seeing those tokens as valid in the context of the importing crate. This distinction is critical because relying on #[macro_export] for cross-crate sharing leads to brittle code that breaks silently until runtime or compilation errors appear unexpectedly, whereas explicitly re-exporting macro tokens ensures predictable behavior and clear error messages when things go wrong.
